text:
Broker Fees: What To Know About The New Law

Beginning on August 1, 2025, a new law in Massachusetts prohibits property owners from requiring renters to pay broker’s fees when they have not hired a broker themselves. Learn more about what this means for your housing search, and how to get help if you’re asked to pay unfair fees.

Effective August 1, 2025: Broker’s fees for rental units must be paid by the person who hired the broker or salesperson. Best bet

Legal tactics: Tenants' rights in Massachusetts, 9 th ed., Massachusetts Law Reform Institute, 2025, with current updates (includes forms). Housing, apartments, shelter, Mass. Legal Help. Various topical collections that contain information and resources relating to landlord-tenant issues, including:
Eviction Rent and security deposits Repairs and bad conditions Taking your landlord to court Utilities And more... Massachusetts laws

Main laws MGL c. 186
 Estates for years and at will (Landlord-tenant law) Related laws MGL c. 40P
 The Massachusetts rent control prohibition act MGL c. 93, § 114 Psychologically impacted properties
Sellers and brokers don't have to tell buyers or tenants about a murder or suicide in the house, or about ghosts or other paranormal activity. However, they can't lie if they are asked about it. MGL c. 112, § 87DDD 1/2
 Real estate brokers and salesmen; persons finding dwelling accommodations for fee Effective August 1, 2025 broker's fees must be paid by the party that hires the broker, which is usually the landlord. MGL c. 140, § 22 Lodging house
A lodging house, or boarding house, is a house that rents out individual rooms to 4 or more people.

MGL c. 112, § 87DDD 1/2
 Real estate brokers and salesmen; persons finding dwelling accommodations for fee Effective August 1, 2025 broker's fees must be paid by the party that hires the broker, which is usually the landlord. MGL c. 140, § 22 Lodging house
A lodging house, or boarding house, is a house that rents out individual rooms to 4 or more people. MGL c. 175, § 99 (Cl. 15th A)
 Standard form and content of policies or contracts insuring against loss or damage by fire or by fire and lightning
An insurance policy for a multi-unit apartment building must provide coverage up to a limit of $750 for each rental unit to cover the actual costs of relocation of any tenant or lawful occupant displaced by fire or by damage resulting from fire. Refer also to:

Law about adverse possession (sometimes called "squatters' rights"). Massachusetts regulations

Department of Public Health 105 CMR 410
 State sanitary code chapter II: Minimum standards of fitness for human habitation
Details the key requirements for housing, including kitchens, bathrooms, electricity, ceiling height, and much more.

Refer also to:

Law about adverse possession (sometimes called "squatters' rights"). Massachusetts regulations

Department of Public Health 105 CMR 410
 State sanitary code chapter II: Minimum standards of fitness for human habitation
Details the key requirements for housing, including kitchens, bathrooms, electricity, ceiling height, and much more. Sections of particular note:
105 CMR 410.260 Means of egress
Explains the responsibilities of both landlord and tenant for the maintenance and clearing of a residence's means of egress (such as stairs, pathways, fire escapes, etc.).
105 CMR 410.420 Habitability requirements
Is your apartment big enough? Includes the requirements for the amount of space per person.
105 CMR 410.550 Elimination of pests
Explains the responsibilities of both landlord and tenant for the control and elimination of pests. Attorney General 940 CMR 3 General regulations 940 CMR 3.17 Landlord-tenant
Regulations for consumer protection from unfair or deceptive acts or practices by landlords. Specifies prohibited acts by landlords in the areas of conditions, notices, rental agreements, security deposits, evictions, and more. Subsection (6)(e) outlines when a landlord may enter the tenant's home. Selected cases

Selected court cases on the topic of landlord-tenant issues

Forms

Landlord-tenant forms available online.

Specifies prohibited acts by landlords in the areas of conditions, notices, rental agreements, security deposits, evictions, and more. Subsection (6)(e) outlines when a landlord may enter the tenant's home. Selected cases

Selected court cases on the topic of landlord-tenant issues

Forms

Landlord-tenant forms available online. Many print legal materials in the law libraries' collections also include forms, and you can submit requests for copies to be sent to you. If you're not sure what you are looking for, contact the law libraries for assistance. Report a code violation

In Massachusetts city and town local health authorities (LHA) enforce the state housing code, 105 CMR 410. "Complaints regarding housing violations must be addressed to the LHA, which may be the board of health, health department, or inspectional services department. The LHA is responsible for conducting inspections, issuing orders to correct, and enforcement to ensure violations are corrected." --
A guide for occupants: Enforcement of the Massachusetts housing code, MA Department of Public Health, 2016. Web sources

General
Attorney General's guide to landlord and tenant rights, Mass. Attorney General. Covers terms of a rental agreement, security deposits and other payments, sanitary code requirements, evictions, and discrimination. Overview of landlord-tenant laws in Massachusetts, Nolo, 2024.

This overview of key landlord-tenant laws in Massachusetts will get you started." Bed bugs and other pests

" As of August 1, 2025, broker’s fees for rental units must be paid by the person who hired the broker or salesperson. If you didn’t hire a broker or salesperson, you shouldn’t be paying their fee.

" As of August 1, 2025, broker’s fees for rental units must be paid by the person who hired the broker or salesperson. If you didn’t hire a broker or salesperson, you shouldn’t be paying their fee. This means that if a landlord chooses to hire a broker or salesperson, they are responsible for paying that broker or salesperson themselves."
Governor Healey, Attorney General Campbell warn against illegal renter-paid broker fees, Mass. Governor and Office of the Attorney General, May 21, 2026. Encourages renters to file complaints against brokers and landlords attempting to charge fees against state law. Complaints
Anyone who thinks they’ve been improperly charged a broker’s fee can contact:
Massachusetts Attorney General’s Consumer Advocacy and Response Division Submit your consumer complaint online, or Call 617-727-8400 for assistance
Mass. Division of Occupational Licensure (DOL)

Apartment Search

What should I know before starting my apartment search? Use our Get Started page to review prices, how to find people, what to know about paperwork, and how to protect yourself to search safely. We will walk you through budgeting, roommate selection, neighborhood options, and understanding leases. How do I find apartment listings? Roommates? Trusted realtors? Northeastern has an online Housing Database with apartment listings near domestic campus locations, sublet listings, roommates, and recommended realtors. Login into the Northeastern Housing Database using your Student Hub login credentials. You will need to create a roommate profile in order to contact other students. Find a roommate (or a room) with someone who shares similar interests, habits, and expectations. Refer to our Roommates page for more information. The Resources Tab also has a list of licensed area realtors that are commonly used by Northeastern students. How much will I have to pay upfront for an apartment? In Boston, you will likely be expected to pay 4 months of rent upfront if working with a broker/realtor. The four payments are as follows: first month’s rent, last month’s rent, security deposit, and a broker/realtor fee. The security deposit and broker fee should not exceed one month’s rent each. Lastly, if a new lock is being put on the unit, a landlord may charge for a new key/lock fee.

In Boston, you will likely be expected to pay 4 months of rent upfront if working with a broker/realtor. The four payments are as follows: first month’s rent, last month’s rent, security deposit, and a broker/realtor fee. The security deposit and broker fee should not exceed one month’s rent each. Lastly, if a new lock is being put on the unit, a landlord may charge for a new key/lock fee. Read more under Rental Costs. What is a broker/realtor fee?

***As of August 1, 2025, new regulation around broker’s fees will take place in Boston. Please refer to the information from mass.gov here ***

Landlords (individuals that own the property) hire brokers to show their apartments to prospective tenants. In Boston, the broker/realtor fee is most often paid by the tenants. This fee is the cost to work with a realtor, agent, or broker. A broker will meet with you to understand what you are looking for (price, location, priorities) and find an apartment that meets your criteria. The broker will show you apartments, answer your questions, and perform an application filling, and credit checks. You do not pay a broker fee until you have found the apartment and agree to submit an application or sign the lease. The broker will draw up the lease and paperwork. Brokers should provide a fee disclosure before the application or lease. Learn more about expectation of realtors and property owners.

You do not pay a broker fee until you have found the apartment and agree to submit an application or sign the lease. The broker will draw up the lease and paperwork. Brokers should provide a fee disclosure before the application or lease. Learn more about expectation of realtors and property owners. Do I need a co-signer for my apartment? Most property managers and landlords require students to have a co-signer. A co-signer agrees to pay the rent if the tenants are unable to do so. Co-signers are often parents or guardians. Co-signers are subject to credit checks or may be asked to provide proof of income. If you do not have a co-signer, you may have difficulty securing an apartment. A landlord wants assurance that you will be able to pay the rent for the entire term. If you have funding documents (bank statements, I-20, DS-2019, or pay stubs) to provide, this may help. It is up to the landlord if they want to accept a tenant without a co-signer as they are not required to do so. You may also want to learn about and use third-party co-signing companies. What is the “No More Than 4” ordinance in Boston? The City of Boston Ordinance known as “No More Than 4” addresses overcrowded apartments. No more than 4 “unrelated persons” may live together in one unit.

It is up to the landlord if they want to accept a tenant without a co-signer as they are not required to do so. You may also want to learn about and use third-party co-signing companies. What is the “No More Than 4” ordinance in Boston? The City of Boston Ordinance known as “No More Than 4” addresses overcrowded apartments. No more than 4 “unrelated persons” may live together in one unit. This ensures that renters are living in safe environments that have enough fire escapes (minimum 2), legitimate bedrooms, and the appropriate square footage per person. All names should be on the lease to guarantee your rights as a tenant. Be wary of any landlord or realtor that does not follow this safety precaution. What is the LightView building on Columbus Ave? I heard that this is for Northeastern students. Yes, Lightview is an off-campus building located within our campus footprint. Lightview an alternative to living on campus. The leasing office is located at 840 Columbus Avenue. Learn more about LightView here. Subletting and Short-Term Rentals

How can I find a sublet (room to rent) or post a sublet (have a room)? Review our subletting page to begin and use the Northeastern Housing Database to search for available sublets. Use these two great features: 1) Housing Search and 2) Roommate Search. Housing Search allows you to filter listings so you only see ‘sublet’ listings posted by fellow students.

Subletting and Short-Term Rentals

How can I find a sublet (room to rent) or post a sublet (have a room)? Review our subletting page to begin and use the Northeastern Housing Database to search for available sublets. Use these two great features: 1) Housing Search and 2) Roommate Search. Housing Search allows you to filter listings so you only see ‘sublet’ listings posted by fellow students. The Roommate Search feature allows you to see students that need someone to fill their room, or fill another room, in their current apartment. You should also make a roommate profile of your own so students can find you when they are searching for someone that “Needs A Room” or “Has a Room.”

I had someone agree to take over my apartment as a sublet, but they have since backed out. Do I have any rights to ensure that they still pay the landlord for the months that they agreed to? Tenant laws in Massachusetts and Boston (and elsewhere) do not necessarily govern subletting practices and documents the same as legal leases. That is, subletting law is minimal and may not be upheld by housing authorities. Therefore, be mindful of who you are subletting to and always have a written sublet agreement between you and the individual moving in. If the subletting agreement is verbal, you have little recourse if the person backs out.

That is, subletting law is minimal and may not be upheld by housing authorities. Therefore, be mindful of who you are subletting to and always have a written sublet agreement between you and the individual moving in. If the subletting agreement is verbal, you have little recourse if the person backs out. If both parties (the tenant and the incoming subletter) signed a sublet agreement that included the date, amount of money to be paid, and to which parties by when, and the original tenant received written permission from the landlord to sublet, you may be able to take the individual who “backed out” to small claims court. Learn more about subletting. How can I find short-term housing (less than 12 months)? View a list of temporary housing locations near campus that are available for one night, and some allow semester-long rentals. The Northeastern Housing Database also has filters for short-term leases and co-living (shared) options that allow you to rent one room within a unit. Lease Inquiries

Can someone help me understand my lease? It’s important to understand the contract that you are signing. You can also educate yourself by reading our Lease Genius website page. After reviewing your lease, fill out the Lease Review form with specific questions for our team! We will provide some insight within 24-48 hours. Which fees are illegal?

Lease Inquiries

Can someone help me understand my lease? It’s important to understand the contract that you are signing. You can also educate yourself by reading our Lease Genius website page. After reviewing your lease, fill out the Lease Review form with specific questions for our team! We will provide some insight within 24-48 hours. Which fees are illegal? The legal fees include first month’s rent, last month’s rent, security deposit, lock fee, and a broker fee (if they are a licensed broker). Questionable and illegal fees include pest removal fees and lease re-signing fees if all tenants are remaining the same. If you are being charged fees you feel are questionable or illegal, contact us via email at offcampus@northeastern.edu or via phone at 617-373-8480. How can I break my lease? It is very difficult to break a lease. You may be able to discuss changing the terms of your lease with your landlord and amend the move out date, however, the landlord is not obligated to grant permission to do so unless there are extenuating circumstances. We recommend contacting our office directly at offcampus@northeastern.edu, 617-373-8480, or visit us in 151 Speare Hall. We can advise you on your rights, your lease terms,  and next steps for your specific situation and needs. What can I do about pests? The landlord is responsible for scheduling and paying for pest removal and treatment.

We recommend contacting our office directly at offcampus@northeastern.edu, 617-373-8480, or visit us in 151 Speare Hall. We can advise you on your rights, your lease terms,  and next steps for your specific situation and needs. What can I do about pests? The landlord is responsible for scheduling and paying for pest removal and treatment. The tenant is responsible for maintaining the unit and keeping it free from garbage and bed-bug infested furniture. Clean apartments attract less pests. It is the tenant’s responsibility to notify the landlord to inform them of any problems, including pest infestations. If pest issues persist even after you have contacted your landlord, make sure to file a report with the Inspectional Services Department. This is a free service provided by the city of Boston for pests and other maintenance issues. What happens to my security deposit? Your landlord must place your security deposit in a separate interest-bearing account. You should receive a receipt for your security deposit upon signing your lease and making initial payments that includes the bank name and account number where the funds are being held.

This is a free service provided by the city of Boston for pests and other maintenance issues. What happens to my security deposit? Your landlord must place your security deposit in a separate interest-bearing account. You should receive a receipt for your security deposit upon signing your lease and making initial payments that includes the bank name and account number where the funds are being held. The landlord must return the security deposit (plus interest accrued) 30 days after the end of  your lease term and may only deduct funds for the following reasons: 1) Unpaid rent, 2) Damages beyond normal wear and tear, 3) Tenant’s percentage of property tax increase (if stated in the lease). If your landlord deducts money for damages, they must provide itemized receipts. More info on the City of Boston’s Housing Stability resource page here. If you do not receive your deposit or disagree with damages, contact your landlord in writing and send the letter via Certified Mail. What can I do to prepare for getting my security deposit back? Always communicate your plan with your landlord. Send an email or write a letter to confirm your last day, key return procedure, and to provide a forwarding address for your security deposit. Follow all cleaning guidelines on your lease.

What can I do to prepare for getting my security deposit back? Always communicate your plan with your landlord. Send an email or write a letter to confirm your last day, key return procedure, and to provide a forwarding address for your security deposit. Follow all cleaning guidelines on your lease. A landlord should not require you to hire a professional cleaning service but if you agree to it in your lease you will need to provide documentation that you paid for a cleaning. Save a receipt for your records. Regardless of what your lease states, you must leave any apartment “broom swept” clean and remove all personal belongings and trash. Schedule a final walk through with your landlord or property manager before you leave so you aren’t caught off guard by repairs or listed damages. Remember, you may only be charged for anything beyond normal “wear and tear.” Take pictures of everything before you move out as well. Review our tips for moving out. What is a Tenant-At-Will lease? This is often called a month-to-month lease where both tenant and landlord agree to terms and payment for a short period of time (typically less than a year). This type of lease allows tenants and landlords to make changes with 30 days notice (examples: raising rent, moving out).

landlord concerns

Who can I speak to about landlord difficulties? We are here to help you navigate a conversation with your landlord.

This type of lease allows tenants and landlords to make changes with 30 days notice (examples: raising rent, moving out).

landlord concerns

Who can I speak to about landlord difficulties? We are here to help you navigate a conversation with your landlord. Contact our OCES team to learn about your rights as a renter. The City of Boston also has resources including the Boston Office of Housing Stability and Inspectional Services Department. You may call 3-1-1 for city support or download the BOS:311 app. Additional city and legal resources can be found under Boston Resources. Can my landlord raise the rent? It depends on the terms of the lease. If you are in a fixed term lease, the landlord cannot raise the rent during the fixed term indicated on your lease. If you are a tenant-at-will, a landlord may raise the rent by giving you 30 days notice. It is typical for a landlord to raise the rent each year if you decide to re-sign, but this would be considered a new lease term. What do I do if I receive an eviction notice? Contact our office for advice on how to proceed. We are available on a drop-in basis or  you may schedule a meeting by calling 617-373-8480 or send us an. Bring all documentation with you (current lease, eviction notice, any emails or messages exchanged with your landlord or property manager).

What do I do if I receive an eviction notice? Contact our office for advice on how to proceed. We are available on a drop-in basis or  you may schedule a meeting by calling 617-373-8480 or send us an. Bring all documentation with you (current lease, eviction notice, any emails or messages exchanged with your landlord or property manager). We will want to gather more information in order to provide you the best advice and refer you to the appropriate resources.

Boston and the surrounding areas of Northeastern offer a wide variety of communities in which to live, each with their own personality and character. Below are some popular neighborhoods. It is important to consider proximity to campus, your rental budget, access to public transportation and type of housing (large building or multi-family home), as this will help determine your preferred neighborhood. More information on Boston’s neighborhoods below and on cityofboston.gov/neighborhoods. Boston Neighborhoods Around Northeastern. Click below to find out more about each. Mission Hill

Home to:
 Brigham Circle Center, Fitzgerald Park, and Longwood medical area. Proximity to Campus:
  1-1.5 miles

T Access:
 Orange line, green line (E line only), 39 bus

Commute to Campus:
 20 minute walk or a 10 minute subway or bus ride. Description:
 Once filled with farms and most of the breweries in Boston, Mission Hill today is a community  of long-time residents and students. It is an architectural landmark district with a combination of single family and three-decker homes. Roxbury

Home to:
 Dudley Square business district and Roxbury Community College

Proximity to Campus:
  1-1.5 miles

T Access:
 Orange line, silver line, local buses. Commute to Campus:
 20-25 minute walk or a 15 minute subway or bus ride.

It is an architectural landmark district with a combination of single family and three-decker homes. Roxbury

Home to:
 Dudley Square business district and Roxbury Community College

Proximity to Campus:
  1-1.5 miles

T Access:
 Orange line, silver line, local buses. Commute to Campus:
 20-25 minute walk or a 15 minute subway or bus ride. Description:
 Home to a great number of parks, schools, and churches, a visitor can see Boston’s history in the architecture and landmarks of the neighborhood. Roxbury is a thriving community with a multitude of housing options nestled among a diverse selection of shops and restaurants. Jamaica Plain

Home to:
 Jamaica Pond, the original J.P. Lick’s Ice Cream, Franklin Park Zoo, Arnold Arboretum

Proximity to Campus:
  2 miles

T Access:
 Orange line, 39 bus

Commute to Campus:
 35 minute walk or a 15 minute subway ride. Description:
 Jamaica Plain is a part of the city of Boston. Diversity is the strength of J.P., to which it is lovingly referred as by residents. The rich diversity in J.P. has created a strong character of social awareness and tolerance among neighbors and residents. It also has many green spaces and walking paths to enjoy! South End

Home to:
 Boston Medical Center, Boston Center for the Arts, Boston Ballet, SOWA market, and InkBlock district.

Diversity is the strength of J.P., to which it is lovingly referred as by residents. The rich diversity in J.P. has created a strong character of social awareness and tolerance among neighbors and residents. It also has many green spaces and walking paths to enjoy! South End

Home to:
 Boston Medical Center, Boston Center for the Arts, Boston Ballet, SOWA market, and InkBlock district. Proximity to Campus:
  1 mile

Commute to Campus:
 10-15 minute walk or a 5 minute subway ride. Description:
The South End, with its blocks of Victorian brick row houses, upscale restaurants and art galleries, has become a very trendy place to live in Boston. Trendy restaurants brush shoulders with coffee shops and local businesses between Tremont St. and Washington St.

Fenway/Kenmore

Home to:
 Fenway Park, The Fens field and community gardens, Isabella Stewart Gardner Museum, Museum of Fine Arts

Proximity to Campus:
  1 mile or less

T Access:
 Green line (B,C, D & E lines), orange line, red line, silver line, local buses. Commute to Campus:
 15-20 minute walk or a 10 minute subway ride. Description:
 The Fenway is more than just Fenway Park. Many new restaurants have opened here recently creating a lot of buzz on this end of Boylston Street. Lansdowne Street continues to have a lot of nightlife near the ballpark.

Commute to Campus:

 The Fenway is more than just Fenway Park. Many new restaurants have opened here recently creating a lot of buzz on this end of Boylston Street. Lansdowne Street continues to have a lot of nightlife near the ballpark. Back Bay

Home to:
 Boston Public Library, Newbury Street, Prudential Center, Public Garden

Proximity to Campus:
  1 mile

T Access:
 Green line (B,C, D & E lines), orange line, many buses. Commute to Campus:
 15-20 minute walk or a 10 minute subway ride. Description:
 Once water behind the Public Garden, Back Bay now holds some of the most exclusive real estate in Boston, diverse and trendy shopping on Newbury Street, and elegant historic brownstone architecture. Back Bay is an exciting place to live that does come with higher rents! More neighborhoods and towns to the north, south, east, and west of the Northeastern University campus include:

Must Know! Avoiding Scams
1. Utilize Northeastern’s Off Campus Database
mentioned in this pamphlet.
2. Stay informed about market rental prices and
exercise caution with listings that appear “too
good to be true” (e.g., unusually low rent).
3. Inspect the apartment in person or through live
video walk-through before making any payments
to ensure its existence and legitimacy of
the lessor.
4. Be cautious of landlords who claim to be abroad
and are unable to meet in person.
5. Refrain from sharing your financial information
with anyone.
6. Avoid using cash, MoneyGram, Venmo, or other
cash apps for payments. Seek assistance from Off Campus Engagement and
Support for any housing concerns. Key Terms
• Addendum: Additional provisions to the lease
document.
• Co-signer/Guarantor: Party responsible for
tenant’s debt in case of rent default.
• Landlord/Lessor: Property owner.
• Lease: Legally binding contract for renting
property.
• Property Manager: Oversees daily property
operations.
• Realtor/Broker: Agent for apartment rental
transactions.
• Security Deposit: Sum paid by tenants before
moving in, refundable within 30 days if no
damages caused. Understanding your Lease International Student
A lease term is typically 12 months. Only sign your
Apartment Guide
lease after fully reviewing and understanding
the clauses.

Understanding your Lease International Student
A lease term is typically 12 months. Only sign your
Apartment Guide
lease after fully reviewing and understanding
the clauses. Must knows to find the right
Common Lease Terms
apartment for you
• Jointly and Severally: All parties have joint liability;
each party is responsible for obligations.
• Legal Tenancy: Only individuals named on the
lease as tenants or lessees are legally permitted to

6 Steps to Protect Yourself from Scams

1. Use the NU Housing Database first! The
Northeastern’s Off-Campus Housing Database is the best option to find local, reputable listings from licensed realtors and property owners. Not only will you save time, but you will be viewing listings from registered users and licensed companies near campus.

2. Research rental costs

It is important to know the average rental costs for the neighborhood. Unusually low rent is a potential sign of a scam. View Boston rental costs by neighborhood.

3. Tour the apartment in-person. This is the best way to confirm the apartment exists! See the apartment in-person, send a proxy, or do a live virtual tour. Scammers will avoid tours as they do not have a property that they will be able to show. Also, use the helpful links below to also confirm the apartments is real by verifying it is a registered rental and looking up the owner in public records.

4. Research the realtor, owner, or property management company. Confirm the realtor office location, phone number, and email and that they are in working order. These should also match the contact info that you have been using to get in touch with the realtor/broker. The links below also can help verify a realtors license. Do not share personal information with any parties until you have confirmed their identity.

5. Get details in writing and read correspondence carefully.

These should also match the contact info that you have been using to get in touch with the realtor/broker. The links below also can help verify a realtors license. Do not share personal information with any parties until you have confirmed their identity.

5. Get details in writing and read correspondence carefully. Details matter! Inquire about fees, due dates, contact information, and the application process. Scammers will often focus on getting your money and will lack many details of the process or not have an application process at all! Make sure to get everything in writing and use our Lease Genius page to double check the lease. The term “refundable” is almost never included in any part of the process.

6. Don’t wire money. Transfer funds securely. Never wire money or use cash as a deposit or payment for the first and last month’s rent. Wiring money is the same as giving cash; you can’t get a refund, even if you find out the offer was a fraud. Avoid using Western Union, MoneyGram, etc. Use methods that can be tracked and protected. The most common and safest form of money transfer for deposits is a bank or using a credit card with fraud protection. View these do’s and don’ts to avoid scams! Resources to Verify Rental Listings

Boston Property Assessment will verify if the address you are looking at exists and who it is owned by.

Avoid using Western Union, MoneyGram, etc. Use methods that can be tracked and protected. The most common and safest form of money transfer for deposits is a bank or using a credit card with fraud protection. View these do’s and don’ts to avoid scams! Resources to Verify Rental Listings

Boston Property Assessment will verify if the address you are looking at exists and who it is owned by. If you find an apartment that does not appear on any maps, this is the site to use to verify it. It will also show the person who owns the property! RentSmart Boston is a free resource that compiles data from BOS:311 and the City’s Inspectional Services Division to give prospective tenants a more complete picture of the apartments they are considering renting. The tool prompts users for an address and generates a report to assist prospective tenants in understanding any previous issues with the property. The Board of Registration of Real Estate Brokers and Salespersons licenses qualified individuals, businesses, and schools who meet the requirements to engage in the practice of real estate brokering and sales in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts. The Board also protects consumers by investigating and disciplining licensee who violate the laws and regulations governing the practice of real estate brokering and sales. Follow the link to verify a realtor license.

The Board also protects consumers by investigating and disciplining licensee who violate the laws and regulations governing the practice of real estate brokering and sales. Follow the link to verify a realtor license. Reporting Scams

Get guidance from Northeastern. Contact Northastern University Police Department’s non-emergency line (617-373-2121) and Off Campus Engagement and Support (617-373-8480) to let us know you have experienced a rental scam. Report the scam to your state consumer protection office. If you lost money or other possessions in a scam, report it to your local police too. Report the incident to the Federal Trade Commission (FTC). FTC is the main agency that collects scam reports. Report the scam to the FTC online, or by phone at 1-877-382-4357 (9:00 AM – 8:00 PM, ET). The FTC accepts complaints about most scams. Report fake websites, emails, malware, and other internet scams to the Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3). Some online scams start outside the United States. If you have been affected by an international scam, report it through econsumer.gov. Your report helps international consumer protection offices spot trends and prevent scams. Book a meeting with us!

Northeastern University is a private research university with its main campus in Boston, Massachusetts, United States. With more than 38,000 students, Northeastern is the largest university in Massachusetts by enrollment! When searching for off‑campus housing near Northeastern University in Boston, students have several vibrant neighborhood options—each with unique perks.Check out the top neighborhoods for incoming Northeastern Students living in Boston.

1. Symphony / Fenway–Kenmore

Location & commute: Situated along Huntington Avenue, just a short walk or MBTA ride to campus. Easily serviced by the Green Line, buses, and nearby Kenmore Square. Vibe & amenities: A mix of student housing and professionals, with access to Fenway Park, shops, and restaurants. Rent & housing: Offers a wide range—from studios to multi-bedroom units—though Fenway–Kenmore tends toward mid-to-high price ranges

2. Back Bay

Walkability: A stylish, upscale area with tree‑lined streets, historic brownstones, boutiques, and cafés—all just a mile or so from campus. Recommended for: Students seeking a more cosmopolitan living experience—but note rents trend on the higher side.

3. South End

Character: Renowned for its Victorian brownstones, leafy parks, art galleries, and a dynamic dining scene. It’s a landmark district loved for its charm. Commute: A pleasant ~15-minute walk or brief MBTA ride to Northeastern.

Recommended for: Students seeking a more cosmopolitan living experience—but note rents trend on the higher side.

3. South End

Character: Renowned for its Victorian brownstones, leafy parks, art galleries, and a dynamic dining scene. It’s a landmark district loved for its charm. Commute: A pleasant ~15-minute walk or brief MBTA ride to Northeastern. Community feel: A vibrant mix of young professionals and students, perfect for those after a cultured yet friendly area

4. Mission Hill

Affordability: Known to be more budget-friendly than Fenway or Back Bay. Transit: Served well by the Green Line (“E” branch) and Orange Line via Roxbury Crossing, making campus commutes manageable. Neighborhood perks: Historic architecture, brick triple-deckers, proximity to Longwood Medical Area, and a diverse local culture with plenty of eateries and conveniences

5. Kenmore

Overlap with Fenway: Often considered part of the broader Fenway–Kenmore area—this neighborhood is ideal for commuters. Student hub: Home to students from Northeastern and Boston University, offering apartment options close to Kenmore Square and the T. Using Off‑Campus Apartment Finder

For Northeastern students, each of these neighborhoods brings something special. Symphony / Fenway and Kenmore offer dynamic student life close to campus. Back Bay delivers upscale living and leisure. South End charms with artistry and beauty.

How Does Off-Campus Housing Near Northeastern Actually Work? [2026 Update] Spot Easy – Apr 1, 2026
For a lot of students, off-campus housing near Northeastern feels confusing at first. You hear people talk about apartment hunting in Boston like it is a race. Someone mentions a lease, someone else warns you about broker fees, and then another friend says you should check the NU housing database before you sign anything. It can all sound more complicated than it should be. The good news is that the off-campus housing process is not random. It moves fast, but it usually follows the same pattern. Students decide they want to live off campus, build a budget, find roommates, compare neighborhoods, search listings, tour apartments, and then sign a lease contract once they find the right fit. Once you understand that sequence, the whole process becomes much easier to manage. If you are wondering how off-campus housing near Northeastern actually works, this guide breaks it down step by step. From apartment search tactics to lease contracts, Boston neighborhoods, sublets, and roommate decisions, here is what students should know before making a move. What Off-Campus Housing Means for Northeastern Students
Off-campus housing simply means living in an apartment, rented house, or sublet that is not operated as part of university housing.

From apartment search tactics to lease contracts, Boston neighborhoods, sublets, and roommate decisions, here is what students should know before making a move. What Off-Campus Housing Means for Northeastern Students
Off-campus housing simply means living in an apartment, rented house, or sublet that is not operated as part of university housing. Instead of living in a residence hall or university-managed space, students rent from private landlords, property managers, or owners in the Boston rental market. For Northeastern students, off-campus housing often becomes part of the normal college experience. Some students move off campus because they want more independence. Others want more space, lower costs compared with some campus options, or more flexibility around co-op schedules. In many cases, students also prefer choosing their own roommates and neighborhoods. There are several housing options available. Some students rent a full apartment with a group of roommates. Others join an existing lease in a shared apartment. Some choose a sublet for a semester or summer. The right option depends on budget, timing, and how long you plan to stay in Boston. The main thing to understand is that off-campus housing is not just one decision. It is a process involving search tools, listings, lease terms, move-in costs, and often multiple people making decisions together.

Others join an existing lease in a shared apartment. Some choose a sublet for a semester or summer. The right option depends on budget, timing, and how long you plan to stay in Boston. The main thing to understand is that off-campus housing is not just one decision. It is a process involving search tools, listings, lease terms, move-in costs, and often multiple people making decisions together. When Students Usually Start the Apartment Search
Northeastern student starting an early apartment search in Boston by researching listings and neighborhoods before winter break
One reason apartment hunting near Northeastern feels intense is that Boston is a competitive rental market. Good apartments can move quickly, especially in popular student neighborhoods. That is why many students begin their apartment search earlier than they expect. In practice, students often start researching months before their target move-in date. Even if they are not ready to sign, they begin comparing neighborhoods, talking to potential roommates, and tracking rent prices. This early research helps them understand what is realistic for their budget. Starting early matters because the apartment search is not just about finding listings. It is also about preparing for fast decisions.

Even if they are not ready to sign, they begin comparing neighborhoods, talking to potential roommates, and tracking rent prices. This early research helps them understand what is realistic for their budget. Starting early matters because the apartment search is not just about finding listings. It is also about preparing for fast decisions. If you wait until the last minute, you may end up with fewer housing options, higher prices, or a location that is not ideal for commuting to campus. This does not mean you should panic and sign the first apartment you see. It means you should understand the housing process early enough to make smart decisions. Students who do well during apartment hunting usually start with research, not rushing. How the Off-Campus Housing Process Actually Works
The off-campus housing process near Northeastern usually follows a clear sequence. First, students decide when they want to move and how long they want to stay. That sounds simple, but it matters because a full lease and a sublet are different commitments. A standard lease may lock you in for a set term, while a sublet may offer more flexibility. Next comes budget planning. Before you seriously search for apartments, you need to know what you can spend each month and what you can afford upfront. A lot of students focus only on rent and forget about total move-in costs.

A standard lease may lock you in for a set term, while a sublet may offer more flexibility. Next comes budget planning. Before you seriously search for apartments, you need to know what you can spend each month and what you can afford upfront. A lot of students focus only on rent and forget about total move-in costs. After that, many students figure out their roommate situation. Some already know who they want to live with. Others use the Northeastern housing database or student networks to connect with roommates. This is a major part of the process because your roommate choice affects apartment size, location, budget, and lease structure. Then comes the actual apartment search. Students use the NU housing database, listing sites, word of mouth, realtors, and student groups to find properties. Once promising apartments appear, students schedule tours, ask questions, compare listings, and review the neighborhood. If they find the right place, the next step is the application and lease review. This is where students often need to move quickly, but they still need to read the lease contract carefully. Signing matters because a lease is a binding agreement, not just a reservation. Finally, once the lease is signed, students prepare for move-in, utilities, furniture, and whatever else the apartment requires. That is how off-campus housing actually works. It is not one giant mystery.

It is not one giant mystery. It is a chain of decisions, and each one affects the next. Using the NU Housing Database and Other Search Tools
One of the most useful starting points for finding off-campus housing is the NU housing database. Many students begin there because it is specifically relevant to the Northeastern community and can help with listings, sublets, and roommate connections. The Northeastern housing database can make the apartment search feel more organized. Instead of searching the entire Boston rental market with no filter, students can focus on options that are more likely to fit their needs. It can also help students discover roommate opportunities or short-term housing options they might otherwise miss. That said, most students do not rely on a single database alone. Off-campus housing searches often include several channels. Students commonly use listing platforms, referrals from friends, neighborhood groups, and realtors.

It can also help students discover roommate opportunities or short-term housing options they might otherwise miss. That said, most students do not rely on a single database alone. Off-campus housing searches often include several channels. Students commonly use listing platforms, referrals from friends, neighborhood groups, and realtors. Some also hear about available apartments through classmates or older students who are moving out. This is where an apartment search toolkit becomes useful. A strong search toolkit usually includes a saved budget range, a list of preferred neighborhoods, a set of must-have apartment features, a way to compare listings, and a few trusted search sources. Apartment hunting becomes much easier when you stay organized. A broad search strategy helps because the best housing options do not always show up in the same place. Some listings appear in databases, some through realtors, and some through direct student connections. How to Build a Realistic Boston Housing Budget
Budget is one of the most important parts of finding off-campus housing, especially in Boston. A lot of students think only about monthly rent, but rent is just one piece of the total cost. A realistic housing budget should include monthly rent, utilities, internet, groceries, transportation, and basic apartment supplies.

How to Build a Realistic Boston Housing Budget
Budget is one of the most important parts of finding off-campus housing, especially in Boston. A lot of students think only about monthly rent, but rent is just one piece of the total cost. A realistic housing budget should include monthly rent, utilities, internet, groceries, transportation, and basic apartment supplies. Depending on the property, students may also need to pay for laundry, furniture, or move-in costs that are easy to overlook. Upfront costs can be even more important than monthly costs. Some apartments require first month’s rent, last month’s rent, a security deposit, and possibly other fees before move-in. That means a seemingly affordable apartment may still be difficult to secure if the upfront payment is too high. When building your budget, it helps to separate costs into two categories: monthly living expenses and one-time move-in expenses. This gives you a more honest view of what you can afford. It is also smart to leave some room for flexibility. The Boston rental market can shift quickly, and the cheapest apartment is not always the best choice if it creates stress in other areas, like commuting time, safety, or roommate compatibility. A good housing decision is not just about getting the lowest rent. It is about finding a place you can realistically afford and live in comfortably.

It is also smart to leave some room for flexibility. The Boston rental market can shift quickly, and the cheapest apartment is not always the best choice if it creates stress in other areas, like commuting time, safety, or roommate compatibility. A good housing decision is not just about getting the lowest rent. It is about finding a place you can realistically afford and live in comfortably. Choosing the Right Boston Neighborhood Near Northeastern
Neighborhood choice shapes the entire off-campus experience. Two apartments may look similar on paper, but the neighborhood can change your daily routine, commute, and overall quality of life. Many Northeastern students look at Boston neighborhoods based on a few core factors: distance to campus, rent level, apartment size, transit access, and general lifestyle. Some students want to be as close to campus as possible. Others are willing to live farther out if it means paying less or getting more space. Popular student areas often balance convenience with cost. Some neighborhoods feel more connected to campus life, while others offer quieter residential living. For some students, being near class buildings matters most. For others, access to grocery stores, public transportation, coffee shops, or green space matters just as much. The key is to think beyond the apartment itself. Ask how the neighborhood fits your real life. How long is the commute?

Some neighborhoods feel more connected to campus life, while others offer quieter residential living. For some students, being near class buildings matters most. For others, access to grocery stores, public transportation, coffee shops, or green space matters just as much. The key is to think beyond the apartment itself. Ask how the neighborhood fits your real life. How long is the commute? Will you feel comfortable walking there? Does the area match your class schedule, co-op needs, and social habits? Does it support your budget over time? Students sometimes focus so much on a property listing that they forget the neighborhood is part of the housing decision. In practice, where you live can matter as much as what you rent. How Roommates Fit Into the Housing Process
For many students, roommates are central to the off-campus housing process. Sharing an apartment can reduce rent and make better housing options possible, but it also introduces more decision-making. Before you start serious apartment hunting, it helps to talk through the basics. What is everyone’s budget? How close to campus does everyone want to live? Does anyone need a quieter space? How will rent be split if the bedrooms are different sizes? Who is willing to take the lead during the search? These conversations may feel awkward at first, but they are much easier than dealing with conflict after signing a lease.

What is everyone’s budget? How close to campus does everyone want to live? Does anyone need a quieter space? How will rent be split if the bedrooms are different sizes? Who is willing to take the lead during the search? These conversations may feel awkward at first, but they are much easier than dealing with conflict after signing a lease. Roommates should discuss expectations early, not after the contract is final. It is also important that everyone reads the lease contract carefully. Some students assume one person can handle the paperwork for the group, but everyone on the lease should understand what they are signing. Lease contracts can affect payment responsibility, move-in rules, and what happens if someone wants to leave early. Good roommates do more than share rent. They communicate clearly during the search, respect deadlines, and stay realistic about what the group can afford. In a fast-moving housing market, strong communication can make the difference between a smooth process and a stressful one. What Happens During Tours, Applications, and Lease Signing
Northeastern student reviewing a Boston apartment lease with potential roommates at a coffee shop before signing
Once you find promising listings, the next stage is tours, applications, and lease signing. This is the point where many students feel pressure to move fast, but it is also where details matter most.

What Happens During Tours, Applications, and Lease Signing
Northeastern student reviewing a Boston apartment lease with potential roommates at a coffee shop before signing
Once you find promising listings, the next stage is tours, applications, and lease signing. This is the point where many students feel pressure to move fast, but it is also where details matter most. During an apartment tour, students should pay attention to more than appearance. Check the actual condition of the apartment. Look at kitchen appliances, bathroom condition, windows, locks, water pressure, storage, and signs of maintenance issues. Think about how the apartment works for daily life, not just how it looks in photos. It is also worth asking practical questions. What utilities are included? When is move-in? How is maintenance handled? Are there laundry facilities? Is the apartment furnished or unfurnished? Who manages the property? These questions can reveal whether a listing is truly a good fit. If you decide to apply, be prepared to move quickly. The application process may involve personal information, proof of income or guarantor details, and other documentation. In a competitive market, delays can cost you the apartment. Before signing, slow down enough to read the lease contract. Understand the term length, payment schedule, renewal rules, and conditions around breaking the lease or subletting.

The application process may involve personal information, proof of income or guarantor details, and other documentation. In a competitive market, delays can cost you the apartment. Before signing, slow down enough to read the lease contract. Understand the term length, payment schedule, renewal rules, and conditions around breaking the lease or subletting. Signing a contract is a serious step. Even if the apartment search has been stressful, do not rush past the lease review stage. The best apartment is not just one that looks good. It is one with a lease you fully understand. Where Sublets Fit Into Off-Campus Housing
Sublets are an important part of off-campus housing near Northeastern because student schedules are not always traditional. Co-op cycles, summer terms, study abroad, and changing plans all make flexible housing valuable. A sublet usually means one person temporarily rents a space from someone who already holds the lease. This can be useful for students who do not want a full-term commitment or who only need housing for a limited period. Sublets can offer flexibility, but they still require caution. Students should verify the arrangement, confirm the dates, understand what they are paying for, and make sure the setup is legitimate. They should also clarify whether utilities, furniture, and building access are included.

Sublets can offer flexibility, but they still require caution. Students should verify the arrangement, confirm the dates, understand what they are paying for, and make sure the setup is legitimate. They should also clarify whether utilities, furniture, and building access are included. For some students, sublets are the easiest way to handle short-term housing needs. For others, they are a backup option when a full lease does not make sense. Either way, sublets are a normal part of the off-campus housing process and worth considering when your timeline does not match a standard apartment lease. Off-Campus Engagement and Student Support
Students do not have to navigate the housing process completely on their own. Off campus engagement resources can help make apartment hunting more manageable by offering tools, guidance, and educational support. These types of resources may include apartment search checklists, budgeting guidance, roommate advice, housing events, and practical information about lease contracts and renter responsibilities. Some students also benefit from apartment fairs or housing-focused campus engagement programming that introduces common housing steps in a more structured way. Even if you plan to search independently, support resources can help you avoid common mistakes.

Some students also benefit from apartment fairs or housing-focused campus engagement programming that introduces common housing steps in a more structured way. Even if you plan to search independently, support resources can help you avoid common mistakes. They can also give you a better understanding of how the Northeastern housing process connects with the wider Boston rental market. Students often think help is only necessary once something goes wrong. In reality, support is most useful before signing, when you are still comparing housing options and trying to understand the process. Common Mistakes Students Make During Apartment Hunting
A lot of stress in off-campus housing comes from preventable mistakes. One common issue is waiting too long to start. Students sometimes assume they can begin searching only when they are ready to sign, but by then they may have fewer choices. Another mistake is underestimating costs. Monthly rent is only part of the housing budget. Upfront expenses and utility costs can change what is truly affordable. Some students also choose roommates too casually. Living with friends can work well, but only if expectations are clear. Budget, cleanliness, schedules, and communication all matter. A major mistake is signing a lease without reading it carefully. A lease contract is not just paperwork. It defines your responsibility and financial commitment.

Some students also choose roommates too casually. Living with friends can work well, but only if expectations are clear. Budget, cleanliness, schedules, and communication all matter. A major mistake is signing a lease without reading it carefully. A lease contract is not just paperwork. It defines your responsibility and financial commitment. Finally, students sometimes focus too much on the apartment and not enough on the neighborhood. A good-looking apartment may still be a poor fit if the location creates an exhausting commute or does not match your daily needs. Final Thoughts on How Off-Campus Housing Near Northeastern Works
So, how does off-campus housing near Northeastern actually work? It works as a step-by-step process. Students research the Boston rental market, decide on a budget, choose roommates, use the NU housing database and other listings, compare neighborhoods, tour apartments, and sign a lease once they find the right match. What makes the process feel overwhelming is not that it is impossible. It is that several decisions happen close together, and each one affects the next. The more prepared you are, the easier apartment hunting becomes. The goal is not just to find any apartment. It is to find housing that fits your budget, your commute, your roommates, and your schedule.
